learning.summary: A multiboot system allows multiple operating systems to be installed on one computer. A bootloader lets the user choose which operating system to start during boot.
learning.explanation:
A multiboot configuration installs two or more operating systems on the same physical computer, typically on separate disk partitions. When the computer starts, a bootloader displays available operating systems and allows the user to select one.

Planning partitions before installation is important because each operating system usually requires its own storage space. Multiboot systems are commonly used for testing software, learning different operating systems, or running applications that require different platforms.

facts.text: A multiboot system contains more than one operating system on a single computer.
A bootloader lets the user choose which operating system to start.
Separate disk partitions are commonly used for each operating system.
Multiboot configurations are useful for testing and compatibility.

examples.text: A computer has Windows for gaming and Linux for development, with a boot menu shown at startup.

examTips: Know that multiboot systems require selecting an operating system during startup.
commonMistakes: Assuming multiple operating systems can share the same installation partition.
scenarios: Recommend a multiboot configuration for a developer who needs Windows and Linux.
pbqIdeas: Plan partitions and identify the correct bootloader behavior.

reviewNotes: Partition planning and bootloader details are curriculum enrichment beyond the explicit transcript evidence.
